attributes.stem: A four-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of fever and rash. He initially developed a low-grade fever two days ago, which his parents treated with paracetamol. They brought him in today as his temperature has now spiked to 38.9°C. A diffuse, erythematous rash appeared around his mouth and groin two days ago and has since spread to involve his arms, legs and trunk. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears irritable but not in acute distress. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.9°C. The rash resembles sunburn, with some skin peeling, particularly in the axillary and groin areas.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Staphylococcus scalded skin syndrome (SSSS) is the most likely diagnosis in this patient. SSSS is caused by an exotoxin-producing strain of Staphylococcus aureus and primarily affects infants and young children. The characteristic signs and symptoms include fever, diffuse erythematous rash, and tenderness with skin peeling, commonly starting around the mouth/flexure areas and spreading to the rest of the body. The rash resembles a scald or burn, hence the name "scalded skin syndrome."

Scarlet fever is caused by group A Streptococcus infection and typically presents with a diffuse erythematous rash that begins around the neck and spreads to the trunk and extremities. However, scarlet fever is usually associated with a "sandpaper-like" rash and the presence of a "strawberry tongue." The absence of these specific findings makes scarlet fever less likely in this case.

Toxic epidermal necrolysis is a severe and life-threatening skin condition usually caused by a reaction to medications. It presents with widespread blistering, epidermal detachment, and mucous membrane involvement. The rash is typically more severe and bullous compared to the diffuse erythematous rash described in this case.

Erythema multiforme is a hypersensitivity reaction that is commonly triggered by infections, medications, or other factors. It presents with target-like lesions that have a characteristic appearance of concentric rings of colour. Although erythema multiforme can involve extensive areas of the body, it usually spares the face and the rash is not typically associated with skin peeling or tenderness, making it less likely in this case. There is also significant mucosal involvement.

Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a severe mucocutaneous reaction often triggered by medications or infections. It typically presents with a prodromal phase of fever and flu-like symptoms, followed by the development of a widespread rash characterized by erythematous macules and target-like lesions. However, there is also significant mucosal involvement seen in Stevens-Johnson syndrome, which is not described in this scenario.

In summary, the presentation of a young child with fever, diffuse erythematous rash resembling sunburn, and skin peeling starting around the mouth and flexures is most consistent with Staphylococcus scalded skin syndrome. The other conditions listed have distinct features that make them less likely in this case.
learningPoints: Staphylococcus scalded skin syndrome (SSSS) typically presents with fever, diffuse erythematous rash, and skin peeling, starting around the mouth and flexures before spreading to the rest of the body.
SSSS primarily affects infants and young children and is caused by an exotoxin-producing strain of Staphylococcus aureus.
The rash in SSSS resembles a scald or burn, hence the name 'scalded skin syndrome'.
references: Brazel M, Desai A, Are A, Motaparthi K. Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome and Bullous Impetigo. Medicina (Kaunas). 2021;57(11):1157. Published 2021 Oct 24. doi:10.3390/medicina57111157.
